entities.description: A 1997 electronic encoding of Westlake's EETS reading text, produced by the University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative from ScanWorx OCR and subsequent markup, proofreading, and review.

notes: The XML header credits ScanWorx OCR, Jason P. Williams for markup, Sean Pollack for proofreading, and Jason Chu for review.

bibliographic.citation: The Prose Life of Alexander from the Thornton MS., ed. J. S. Westlake, Early English Text Society, Original Series 143 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1913, for 1911).
bibliographic.publisher: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 143
bibliographic.title: The Prose Life of Alexander from the Thornton MS.

entities.description: The EETS reading text on printed pp. 1–115. It combines Westlake's transcription of the surviving Middle English witness with his Modern English translations from an unidentified Latin text at the lost opening and two internal lacunae.

identifiers.scheme: Early English Text Society Original Series

entities.label: J. S. Westlake's The Prose Life of Alexander from the Thornton MS.
notes: The prefatory note says that illness prevented Westlake from revising and publishing the text himself; E. M. Thompson collated it against the manuscript and John Munro read the proofs.
The promised introduction, notes, and glossary were reserved for a future volume that was not published.
Westlake's Latin translations supply missing narrative and are editorial English, not reconstructed Middle English.

rights.basis: The work was published in 1913 and the U-M/Internet Archive records treat the source as public domain or not in copyright in the United States.
rights.component: 1913 edition text and Westlake's editorial matter

entities.description: The complete mid-fifteenth-century paper miscellany written by Robert Thornton. Its first component is the unique surviving copy of the Middle English Prose Life of Alexander at ff. 1r–49r.
entities.label: Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, complete Lincoln Thornton Manuscript
notes: This whole-codex entity allows non-PLAlex images and the complete Scolar facsimile to remain distinct from the ff. 1r–49r work component.

physicalDescription.extent: 322 leaves in current numbering; original minimum approximately 340 leaves
notes: The complete codex is attributed to the single scribe Robert Thornton.
The catalog says it was written by and for Thornton of East Newton, North Yorkshire.

entities.description: Robert Thornton's sole surviving copy of the Middle English Prose Life of Alexander, the first item in the Lincoln Thornton Manuscript. The component begins imperfectly and has internal losses after ff. 18v and 19v.

holding.notes: The Prose Life is cataloged as item 1 at ff. 1–49 and closes with the explicit transcribed in both the catalog and Westlake.

entities.label: Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, Prose Life of Alexander component
notes: The catalog states that the manuscript was written by and for Thornton of East Newton, North Yorkshire. That association does not prove the anonymous translation was composed there.
The witness is unique, but the anonymous work is not identical to the physical codex or to Thornton's scribal act.

physicalDescription.extent: A three-quire booklet occupying ff. 1r–49r within the complete codex
notes: At least one leaf is missing between ff. 18v and 19r, and at least one between ff. 19v and 20r; catchwords and narrative discontinuity establish the losses.

rights.basis: The medieval text and manuscript copy date to c. 1430–1450.
rights.component: Underlying fifteenth-century Middle English text

rights.basis: Images are requestable for a fee, and the site's terms reserve rights and distinguish personal reference use from licensed commercial use; no PLAlex reproduction order was placed.

next_steps: Consult specialized source studies and textual collations across J3 manuscripts and early prints.
Do not infer an exemplar from broad recension agreement or Westlake's isolated citation of the 1489 and 1494 prints.
notes: The J3 recension relationship is supported, but its textual variation prevents a physical-exemplar claim from the consulted evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which physical Latin exemplar or exemplars underlie the anonymous Middle English translation and Westlake's three Modern English supplies?

next_steps: Ask Lincoln Cathedral Library whether a complete internal digitization or priced PLAlex image set exists.
Recheck institutional IIIF and manuscript portals before a future review.
notes: The negative result concerns public access only; the institution may hold unpublished images.
openQuestions.question: Is a complete public digital facsimile of Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, or at least ff. 1r–49r, available?

next_steps: Seek a current content-level rights statement from U-M or the CME repository maintainer.
Keep the three statements layer-separated until their applicability is documented.
openQuestions.question: How should the pinned XML's legacy redistribution restriction, current U-M No Copyright–United States label, and CME repository Unlicense be reconciled for reuse of the exact artifact?

next_steps: Review dialectal and linguistic studies of the Prose Alexander independently from codex production evidence.
Do not substitute Thornton's scribal location for the work's place of composition.
openQuestions.question: Can the anonymous Middle English translation be dated or localized more narrowly than its attestation by the c. 1430–1450 East Newton-associated witness?

next_steps: Inspect surviving Westlake papers or unpublished editorial materials if located.
Keep the current consulted relations scoped to the one explicit note.
openQuestions.question: Did Westlake use the 1489 or 1494 Strasbourg editions beyond the single explicit p. 6 note, and which physical copies did he consult?

reviewNotes: The cataloged intellectual subject is one anonymous Middle English prose romance, distinct from the sole manuscript copy, Westlake's composite reading text, the U-M encoding, and the repository XML artifact.

Middle English is the work's primary language. Modern English occurs only in Westlake's representation-level supplies, while Latin occurs in the source-tradition relationship, three embedded verse couplets, and the explicit.

The eight DIV1 containers are edition or encoding sections, not asserted intrinsic chapters. The work remains prose for discovery because all eight L elements form one brief embedded prophecy cluster at printed pp. 94–95 / manuscript f. 40r.
The witness is acephalous and has at least one leaf missing after f. 18v and at least one after f. 19v. Westlake's opening and internal supplies are not represented as surviving Middle English text.

The 1489 and 1494 Strasbourg prints postdate the c. 1430–1450 witness. Westlake explicitly names both only in a selected note on printed p. 6; neither is asserted as his general supply text or as the medieval translator's exemplar.

No complete public digital facsimile of Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 was verified on 2026-07-15. The one public catalog image is part of f. 80r, outside the PLAlex component, while the complete Scolar photographic facsimile is cataloged for onsite consultation at Wellcome Collection.

Current U-M discovery metadata and the current Compendium XML label the item public domain / No Copyright–United States, while the pinned XML embeds a narrower personal, research, and teaching notice. Their relationship and the applicability of the CME repository's software-worded Unlicense remain unresolved.

The current Compendium XML and pinned artifact preserve identical Unicode-alphanumeric-normalized BODY text when the current file's two CORR alternatives are excluded in favor of its SIC readings. Their wrappers and element vocabulary differ, so they are modeled as versions rather than byte-identical files.

All eleven cached public source files were checked by checksum and byte count. The three PDFs passed qpdf integrity checks; both ZIP files passed archive tests; all three cached XML files are well formed; the Heidelberg bundle contains its canonical IIIF manifest, inventory, and all 74 listed image files.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a pinned 1997 University of Michigan encoding of J. S. Westlake's 1913 EETS reading text. Westlake transcribed the unique, acephalous and internally defective Middle English witness in Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, ff. 1r–49r, and supplied the missing opening and two internal lacunae in Modern English from an unidentified Latin text. The medieval work is an anonymous translation-adaptation of the variable Latin Historia de preliis J3 recension, but no physical Latin exemplar has been identified. Later Latin incunabula and modern editions are therefore modeled only at their evidenced consultation or comparison scope, never as the medieval translator's exemplar.

catalogingSubject.label: The Prose Life of Alexander
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English prose Alexander romance uniquely attested, in an acephalous and internally defective copy, at Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, ff. 1r–49r. The intellectual work is distinct from Robert Thornton's manuscript copy, J. S. Westlake's 1913 EETS edition and its Modern English lacuna supplies, the 1997 University of Michigan encoding, and this repository's pinned XML artifact.

contentParts.summary: A short prophecy exchange embedded in the prose narrative: three Latin couplets spoken by the trees and Alexander's Middle English couplet asking who will kill him.

assertion.notes: The witness supplies only a terminus by attestation; it does not establish a precise composition year.

dateStatements.display: Before or by the sole extant witness, c. 1430–1450; exact translation date unknown

notes: This dates the extant copy, not necessarily the translation.

extent.description: One continuous prose work without securely established intrinsic numbered sections. Westlake's reading text occupies printed pp. 1–115; the manuscript portion is at Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, ff. 1r–49r, with an acephalous opening and losses after ff. 18v and 19v. The pinned XML has eight editorial/encoding DIV1 containers, 178 P elements, 114 PB elements marking printed pp. 2–115, 98 manuscript milestones intended to represent f. 1r through f. 49r but including one malformed f. 5v marker and a duplicate f. 19 marker at a supply boundary, eleven ADD elements in three supply zones, six retained NOTE1 elements, and one embedded prophecy cluster of four Q1 verse groups and eight L elements at printed pp. 94–95 / manuscript f. 40r. The cluster consists of three Latin couplets and the Middle English couplet 'Tell me now, haly tree / Wha he es þat sall slaa mee'; it does not change the whole-work prose discovery form.

formStatements.description: Continuous prose romance with one brief trees-of-the-sun-and-moon prophecy episode containing four encoded verse groups: three Latin couplets and one Middle English couplet, eight L elements total. Under the catalog's form rule, this short quoted cluster remains part-scoped and does not make the otherwise sustained prose work mixed-form.

genres.label: Translation and adaptation
notes: The work descends from the J3 recension of the Latin Historia de preliis, but its physical exemplar is not identified.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The pinned XML header's sole LANGUAGE entry, Latin, is incorrect for the body as a whole.
Middle English also supplies one embedded two-line question within the trees-of-the-sun-and-moon prophecy.
The cataloging boundary is one anonymous Middle English work, not the whole Lincoln Thornton codex and not Westlake's composite editorial representation.

The surviving witness is imperfect at the beginning and after ff. 18v and 19v. Westlake's three Modern English supply zones remain explicitly editorial and are not assigned Middle English work-language status.
The eight XML DIV1 containers follow the source edition and encoding; they are not asserted as an intrinsic eight-chapter organization.

The 1489 and 1494 Strasbourg editions postdate the c. 1430–1450 witness. Their documented use by Westlake for one supplied-opening note does not make either print the translator's exemplar or the identified source of every supplied passage.

next_steps: Review current linguistic work on the Prose Alexander independently of Thornton's copying dialect.
Keep the witness's East Newton and North Yorkshire associations separate from the work's place of composition.
Do not convert the witness date into an exact composition date.
openQuestions.question: Can the anonymous Middle English translation be dated or localized more narrowly than before or by its c. 1430–1450 sole witness?

next_steps: Compare the Middle English readings against current J3 manuscript and print recensio work.
Preserve the J3 work-level relation without promoting the later 1489 or 1494 Strasbourg prints to medieval exemplars.
Record any proposed exemplar only with provenance and reading-level evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which physical J3 exemplar, if any extant object, underlay the Middle English translation?

subjects.label: Magic, prophecy, disguise, and supernatural knowledge
Marvelous peoples, animals, islands, and natural wonders
The Eastern Mediterranean, Near East, Central Asia, and India

summaries.value: This anonymous Middle English prose romance recounts Alexander's legendary conception and childhood, his rise to Macedonian kingship, wars against Darius and Porus, world conquest and journeys among marvelous peoples and creatures, correspondence with rulers and Brahmans, prophecies of his death, poisoning, last testament, and burial. It adapts the J3 recension of the Latin Historia de preliis. The only surviving Middle English copy is acephalous and has two internal lacunae.

PLAlex represents Westlake's complete reading text on printed pp. 1–115 rather than a homogeneous manuscript transcription. Modern English translated supplies precede the surviving manuscript opening and fill losses after ff. 18v and 19v; the remaining text derives from Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, ff. 1r–49r. The XML omits the 1913 prefatory note and most printed apparatus while retaining six notes.

notes: The possessive association identifies Thornton's unique scribal copy; it does not attribute authorship to him.
